title: Georgina Ford -Director's Interview - Megan | Managing Editor
meeting_title: Georgina Ford -Director's Interview - Megan | Managing Editor

transcript.text: Hey, Georgina.
How are you doing?
Good, thanks.
How are you?
Good, good.
Yeah, yeah.
I guess only two note takers in the meeting, so not too bad.
Sometimes we have like five note takers in the meeting.
Yep.
Often on client meetings when there aren't 18 people, there's 18 note takers.
Exactly.
Yeah, well, yeah, super looking forward to chatting.
Yeah, just to introduce myself.
So I'm a director here at GrowthX.
I've been here at the company since about May.
And I, yeah, I'm in a very client-facing role.

I manage about, I think I'm at 11 accounts now and then manage the team of, let's see, I have, I have three managing editors and then they have a whole batch of content managers that are working on the workflows.
So, yeah, just excited to sort of hear, hear from you and get to do you a little bit better.
Great to meet you.
I am a senior account director at Unreal Digital.
I have about 20-ish clients right now.
Oh, well, okay.
Yeah, we do a lot.
Well, how's that doing?
Yeah, so I have Google.
Google Cloud, AMD, Palo Alto, Sage, and then some smaller clients, but those are kind of my big growth accounts.
Yeah.
And then we do a lot of Google partners.
So I guess you can't really call them their own independent clients, but they're like partner clients, so they're still clients.
You know, they're just using Google Cloud MDF funds.
Okay.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Nice.
Cool.
Awesome.
Well, yeah, I know that you've already spoken with a few folks in this process.

So, yeah, I really just have a few questions for you, just like kind of wanting to better understand like execution and managing multiple accounts, which it sounds like you are already doing with your 20 or so clients and, you know, quality control and just like collaboration.
Yeah, so I guess just to sort of jump right in, I was hoping if you could describe a time when you were managing multiple projects or accounts with like.
Competing deadlines and how you sort of organized your work.
In my current role, so again, it's situation specific, but in my current role, I have to prioritize my high value clients.
So big dollar clients.
And I know different companies have different parameters, but that's our company parameter.
And then if I'm looking at my team, writing, design, and my managers as well, I have to be careful to make sure that they have space in their time, in their calendars to actually do the work.
So I spend a lot of time communicating across the team.
And then if we can't, we actually just use contractors.
We expand the team, we put in a contractor to make sure that we can make those deadlines.
If not, we communicate to the client and say, hey, we've had a slight interaction.
No delay.
Can we push this out a week?
If it's a hard no, I do it myself.
Yeah, for sure.
Yeah, I know how that goes.
Yeah, definitely.
I'm just like, okay, I guess I'm doing this task.
And so what kind of tools do you use to sort of like manage and track all of the various deliverables?
So initially we had Asana and I set up, sorry, I can't speak today, set up custom content creation and project management flows in Asana because we didn't have anything when I first joined.
So I had to go and everyone was just chaotically doing everything.
And then the creative team had no idea when stuff was coming in.
that writing and design and people were just like, hey, can you finish this in three hours?
And they were like, ah.
So there's a lot of panic going on.
So I created custom flows in Asana, which took into consideration, we usually give the client sort of 40%.
Five days feedback time, so they can review the asset and get back to us within a five-day period.
And then the creative team usually has five days to turn that around and send it back to me.
I then review it to make sure it meets standards, make sure it meets the client editorial guidelines and design guidelines.
And then once I've checked it, confirmed, made sure nothing needs to be updated or changed, then I send it out to the client.
So that was all incorporated within the Asana timelines project management that I created.
And also in Asana, what I liked was you could share the public timeline with a client.
And they couldn't see who was doing what, but they can see if they delay for two days, they could see how the ramifications slowed down.
So you could say, hey, look, now your delivery date, we'll do our best to meet the original, but here's what's happened with delays.
So I did that in Asana, and then the whole agency...
So over to a new, more manual platform called Ravetree.
So I redid the process in this new platform.
So that's basically what I've been doing is creating and managing that.
Okay, cool.
Awesome.

Yeah, and so, and I guess just, yeah, in managing all these accounts, like, have there, I guess, yeah, could you talk a little bit about when some things have maybe started to fall through the cracks or it kind of sounds like, I mean, I guess you answer that, you're, you, you end up doing it.
You end up doing it yourself if something's going to fall through.
Yeah, that's exactly what happens is I, so I pride myself, I've been doing content for a very long time.
So I have never missed a deadline.
In the 20 years that I've been doing content, I've never missed a deadline and I don't plan to.
Obviously, if there is, I've never.
Had a situation, actually, where I haven't been able to deliver.
I mean, it might not be 12 noon, it might be 5 p.m., but I'll get it to them on the day, no matter what the circumstances are, because that's the client expectation.
And I used to work in publishing.
So you can't say to the printer, hey, I'm not going to get it to you tomorrow, maybe next week, you know?
Right.
You don't get it there, and they're like, well, it sucks to be you.
For sure.
Okay, cool.
Awesome.
Yeah, and so could you, yeah, I mean, you've obviously been in content for, yeah, 20 years or so.
And so can you tell me about a time where, like, you caught a major issue with content before it shipped to the customer?
Maybe it sounds like you've caught many.
And so what did you catch?
How did you catch it?
And then what did you do?
I'm going to tell you my most epic failure story.
So.
It wasn't necessarily my most epic failure, but it was probably the company's most epic failure.
So I was working for ITP, which is a big publishing house in Dubai.
They've got about 70 magazines and associated digital social events, right?
And one of the events that they were managing was the launch of the Burj Khalifa, which is the world's tallest building.
And originally it was going to be called the Burj Dubai, I think.
I can't remember which way around that is.
So 9 p.m., once we've printed all the collateral with the name on, 9 p.m., we get a call from the managing editor or the editorial director.
And he was like, they've decided to change the name because Dubai government couldn't finish paying for the final building of the Burj Dubai.
So Abu Dhabi, the neighboring emirate, has basically...
Bought it.
So we need to change every single piece of collateral and reprint it before six o'clock tomorrow morning.
Oh my God.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So how did you, how did you get through that?
Just got the teams.
Everyone got a bonus, a large one, because we had to work overnight.
Right.
So my role, I had about 15 creatives and I went, they were all updating various design assets, everything from printed napkins, you know, like really.
And then going through and checking every single PDF for every single asset for all of those 20 creatives.
And then once I'd confirmed them, they were sent out and printed.
And I don't think we missed one.
Or if they did, they got burned somewhere.
But as far as I know, we got them all right.
Right.
Um...
Right.
But there are things like more recently, obviously, and less catastrophic or potentially catastrophic than that.
We've had clients that are maybe not as happy with the quality.
And I feel like in a lot of cases that it doesn't matter if you put out the best quality.
It's not necessarily what their idea of it that they have.
So when it's happened, I have found the most successful way of fixing that problem is to get on a live call with them and say, okay, what is wrong with this piece of content?
Where do you think it's failing?
Let's write it together.
What do you think it should be doing?
What do you think it should be saying?
Okay, but maybe if you put it this way, make it more active and not passive, that might meet your audience needs better and things like that.

So I will get on a call and I will walk them through the asset and make sure, however long it takes, that when I get off that call, we've got a solid place to look at it again, just make sure all the grammar, spelling, and all...
But little things are right to send them.
For sure.
Nice.
And with the previous example, with having to change all the assets, like working overnight, I'm curious, how did you go about communicating that with your team and getting them all on board?
Because I'm sure no one was happy.
Yeah, no one was happy.
Luckily, it sounds like a boast, but it's not really.
Luckily, I've always had a very good relationship with the teams that I work with.
So, you know, if they need time to go and take their kid to a football game or whatever, I'm always like, absolutely.
I trust you to do your job.
And, you know, you can do whatever you need to do as long as you provide the product that I need.
So, I've always had a very good relationship with my teams, which has...
here And hat To analysis,.断 I imagine.
dude.
I've never had voluntary turnover in my teams.
I've had downsized, not from me, from the senior leadership, but I've never had someone go, I hate you, I'm leading.
So I was able to speak to them and say, hey, look, you're going to be getting a bonus.
We all like a bonus.
We're going to look like idiots if we don't fix this.
And it's nobody's fault.
Let's pull together and see what we can do.
And we can manage it.
And everyone's willing to come in.
Just throw our hands in the air and roll our eyes a little bit and make sarcastic comments about, well, didn't realize Dubai was so broke.
know.
For sure.
Cool.
Awesome.
Thank you.
And so, let's see.
And now, yeah, I just want to talk a little bit more about, like, collaboration with, like, leadership.
I've, yeah, I've heard a lot about, like, yeah, kind of collaborating with, like, people who are reporting to you.
But so, could you talk about a time when...

A boss maybe gave you critical feedback on whether it was like a process you had in place, a priority, or like a deliverable, and just, yeah, what was that feedback, and then how did you respond to it?
Let me think.
Yeah.
I guess I haven't really had a situation where I've had negative or less than ideal feedback from the leadership team, obviously from clients, that's part of the job sometimes.
So, I mean, do, I guess I do a lot of collaboration with leadership and my team to make sure that we're all aligned and going down the right path.
I've tried experiments that maybe haven't.
It but then I'm like, oopsie, okay, we tried a thing, didn't work.
How can we make it better?
But it's never, I've never really had a situation where I've had feedback that, I mean, I'm a lifelong learner.
So if someone is like, this is terrible, you're like, okay, why?
How can I fix it?
I'm not going to, I'm not someone who gets all petty, like, oh my God, that's mine.
I wrote it.
But she can't say horrible things about it, you know?
Yeah, for sure.
So, I mean, I know that was a terrible answer to that question.
I honestly, right now, I can't think of any situation where I've had that kind of happen.
Yeah, and I mean, not even, like, not even critical and, like, you did a bad job.
But, like, even just, like, okay, so, like, what was the experiment that you, like, you did and, like, maybe failed?
And then, like, could you tell me a little bit more about that?
I was trying...
trying...
To do, so we use a content syndication partner, and we've had a challenge recently where the leads have not matched the ICP at all.
Okay.
So I was trying to do, part of what I do is I create AI processes and like AI, like gems or GPTs and things to help our team more efficiently use their time.

So I had a brilliant idea of creating a GPT to look at the spreadsheets and match them against the ICP and the target account list, and then get it to tell me how many actually matched, how many matched five criteria, four, three, two, one, zero criteria.
And it didn't work.
I was so excited about it, you know.
So that's an ongoing work in progress.
And I...
I don't know why it's not working, and the leadership team was, not because, they didn't ask for it.
I kind of went, I'm going to try this thing, and then I was like, okay, it didn't work, so ignore that.
Yeah, for sure.
Awesome.

Yeah, let's see, I mean, those are, yeah, I think those are really like the core questions that I had for you, but did you, do you have any questions for me, or just like anything else you want to add that you feel like I should, I should know?
I have some questions.
Yeah.
What do successful client relationships look like to grow with AX?
Yeah, I mean, successful relationships, like we're, we're kind of looking at it across a few different parameters.
So like, we're looking at overall relationships.
Um, how much like the client trusts us, how much the client takes our feedback into account, like how much pushback there is.

And like, there's still part of us that like, we are, we are also trying to define our ICP and like, cause like there were definitely like some clients we had earlier that were, um, that we actually, we fired them because we just determined that they weren't a good fit for us.
And so that's obviously like a better place to be in.
Um, but so relationship, and then there's also like content quality.
Um, there is, uh, performance.
Um, and then I'm blanking on, yeah, we actually just recently came up with like an account help matrix to basically determine like, how are we performing in these core areas?
And like, ultimately, like we are paying a lot of attention to churn, but also paying attention to like renewal.
We're like, we want to make sure that we are doing.
Everything to ensure that, like, that these clients are continuing to get value from us and actually want to continue working with us, want to actually renew their contracts for another year.
And so we've been around for about a year now.
And so, like, we're actually having, like, our first batch of renewals coming up.
So that's, like, sort of, like, a big focus for us in ensuring, like, because we're, like, it's not going to work if people don't want to renew with us.
So it's, like, we're really trying to, like, get clear on, like, okay, like, what are these parameters where we can make sure we improve?
So we're actually, like, in the process of more formalizing that account review, that account of review process to make sure that we are best set up for success in the long term.
Okay.
What distinguishes your approach to client management from other agencies, would you say?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I mean, I think so at its core, it's like we are ultimately not an agency.
We are an AI workflow company, but even that framing has evolved.
I do think when we first started, we were more of like a services agency that used AI.
And now within like the last few months, like we actually have in-house like our own AI workflows product.
And the idea is that as we move forward, like we will actually just offer that tool to clients.
So like there will essentially be like two ways to work with growthx.
There's, you can just use our, you can just use our AI workflows product and that'll come with our in-process.
Engineering is currently working on this right now, like our in-house analytics and performance dashboard.
So it can just be entirely self-serve.
Or.

Or if you want a little bit more hands-on with the actual content strategy, then there's, like, still at its core, it's the physical product, but you still have that support from the account strategist and the managing editors, et cetera.
So I think that's, like, the core differentiation is that, like, we are not actually, like, your traditional agency, like, services sort of company.
But, like, we are truly, like, AI first.
are our product first.
And then the strategy, the more, like, human interaction, that's, like, an add-on versus, like, the core offering.
Okay.
You were mentioning analytics and stuff.
I was wondering, I've been seeing a lot on LinkedIn and various, Reddit and everything, where people are looking for some way to measure AEO.
So I was wondering.
I'm wondering if you guys are doing that.
Because that's like a big thing right now.
It is.
Yeah.
We are.
So right now we're using a tool called Scrunch.
Are you familiar with Scrunch?
Okay.
Yeah.
And there's also some clients actually are already independently using like Profound, which is just a Scrunch competitor.
And so at this point, yeah, like we're using those tools to track prompts.
Basically, like we have our own process for determining which prompts we should even be tracking based on the ICP for each specific client.
But then on top of that, we're also tracking just LLM referrals, just using, yeah, using GA4, using GSE.
But then we kind of have it all built into these.
We use Looker right now.
But the internal tool that we're working on, we'll combine all of those into one platform.
So that we can more, just so everything.
It's all in one place and we can actually then like own that, own that reporting.
But, but yeah, yeah, AO or GEO or whatever, whatever you want to call it is like.
acronym you feel like this week.
Yeah, it's like, it's definitely like an evolving space and one that like, we're all, you just like see everyone trying to figure it out in real time.
Everyone's trying to figure out like, okay, like, how can we really like take advantage of this and make it work for us?

And like, I think what's been interesting to see is like, I don't know, it's, it's tough because there's still the data, there's no data available on what people are actually typing into, into these LLMs.
So like, it's a lot, it's a lot of guesswork, but it's still at least like something that we've been trying to do for clients is at least like, so we have a process for determining the prompts.
And then from there, it's like, okay, really checking where competitors are performing.
like, like, like, you.

And seeing like okay like is there like are there what are the clear areas where like maybe a competitor is outperforming our client and then really zooming in on what's being cited and then like to like looking at like okay well like is it like a blog post from a competitor that's like really outperforming and then and then you kind of like almost like back your way you kind of go back into SEO because you're like okay well like ultimately the LLMs are they're they're searching the internet so like okay like what are what are they finding on the internet and like is there an opportunity for us to do a similar piece of content that may maybe like bump that competitor off from from that citation.

So um yeah so that's just to say we are actively trying to make sense of the landscape just just as much as everyone is right it's exciting though because there's so much you don't know and there's so much you know So is if we're
And when you do content as well, is it mostly traditional channels?

So your blogs and your, I'm not going to say white papers, because nobody really likes white papers anymore, except one of my clients, NVIDIA, who thinks the world revolves around white papers, which is G-books, that kind of stuff.
Or are you utilizing different channels or creating content for like Reddit and things like that?
Yeah, so our bread and butter, our blog post, that said, we, I have a couple of clients where we're doing, we're doing LinkedIn posts for them.
So like, we have our AI workflows come up with like, a LinkedIn version, and then a blog post version.
I know, not with my accounts, but with a colleague's account, they are doing a Reddit experiment.
So like, we're definitely starting to explore other, other, other media channels, but the core.
There's definitely a blog post.
And then I guess I actually do have another account where we're doing some landing page refreshes with them.
But yeah, 95% of what we do are blog posts, at least for right now.
Okay.
And when you say blog posts, how do you define those?
Because there's different ideas of a blog post or there's different iterations of blog posts.
You have the more timious ones that could almost be PR, but aren't really.
And then there's the ones that are kind of more product pitches.
And then there's the thought leadership.
Do you do across the spectrum?
Do you focus on specific tasks?
Yeah, we do across the spectrum.
But typically, it's a lot of more informational.
And then kind of like talking about how the client's products can support those.
Whatever those needs are.
But we also do some thought leadership content as well.
And then for one specific client, we're doing more like product, more like essentially like product blogs, like talking specifically about features.
So like, it's really just like, it's dependent on the client.
But like we, like I have a client that is, it's an ed tech startup that uses AI in schools.
And so like some of the content we're doing is like, kind of like debunking some myths around AI and like why AI isn't scary.

And like, and things like that, just like get, help people feel more comfortable with AI, like helping teachers and administrators feel more comfortable sort of actually thoughtfully bringing AI into, into schools, into classrooms, how to, how to work with students on that.
How to make sure that like students are educated about like how AI works and like how they can use it and then maybe how they shouldn't use it.
So a lot of it is just client-dependent.
Okay.
And what would you say the biggest barrier to growth is right now for the company?
When I was interviewing with – I'm sorry.
I've just had a complete blank moment.
You mentioned that you don't have enough staff for the amount of clients that you have or you want more staff for more clients.
Is that one of the challenges, would you say?
Or having more staff?
Yeah.
Yeah, it's – I think the biggest issue is that we are still perfecting the product itself.
So like I said, it's like we are trying to be like AI product first.
But the way it is right now is that there is
Is still a lot of human input that is required to make sure we are getting the best out of those workflows.
ideally, once the product has been truly perfected, then myself and managing editors and other folks will have, everything will just be a lot easier.
And so right now, there's a good amount of troubleshooting that happens with the workflows and more editing than I think anyone would really want to have to be doing.
And so I think, not to put it all on product, but I do think...
It's all their fault!
It's not us, it's them.
But I do think there's a little bit of that.
But there's also, there's also, we on the, so there's basically like the build side of the company, and then there's the delivery side.
So like, I'm on the delivery side.
Delivery side, he would be on the delivery side.
And so on this side, it's still also about like, we are currently rethinking ways to sort of like divvy up responsibilities.

Like in some ways there is like, there is too much like overlap of work, overlap of skills and like trying to figure out how to best put people where they will do the best work and like do the work that they're most excited about.
Like some folks on our team, like there are some managing editors on our team who like love working with clients and others who are just like, I would love it if I like never had to talk to a client.

So like figuring out how to just like, okay, like we're going to have them just like be really focused on like the workflows and editing and internal like performance tracking and then they can just scan that information off.
So it's like, we're also rethinking ways to just like make the structure of the company just work a little bit more like seamlessly.

And we're also just trying to, like, buy people back some time and, like, not have, like, two people from delivery team on every, like, 30-minute weekly client call versus, like, well, maybe just only one of us needs to be there.
And so, like, I'm really looking forward to that part.

So just, like, getting some time back to actually, like, be able to take that, like, step back at that bigger picture and make sure that we're delivering for folks across the board, especially as, like, a lot of these renewals are coming up.
So, yeah, I would say, yeah, that's just, yeah, also trying to take some responsibility on the delivery side, for sure.
That's interesting.
I know the company is a year old.
And you say, obviously, there's still a lot going on and trying to find what works and moving things around to make it work.
So is innovation and experimentation still kind of something?
Anything that is encouraged or allowed, probably the wrong word, within the teams?
Yeah, absolutely.
Yeah, like I even just like, yeah, before I hopped on this call with you, I was experimenting in our, so our product is called Atlas.
So I was just experimenting with building my own workflow for a client.
Just recognizing that like engineering is really strapped right now.
They're doing a lot of things.
They're working on rolling out our agentic workflows.
They're working on the performance dashboard.
And so I was just like, okay, let me like play around with like some of these pipelines.
Let me just see if I can like make this work for the deliverable that I need my team to work on.
So like there's, yeah, experimentation is definitely encouraged.
Maybe even a prerequisite.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, you need to be, like, very willing and able to experiment, honestly.
And, like, and, yeah, it's, is that something that, it sounds like you like to experiment?
I have, yeah.
And I don't know if you guys use, like, gems or GPTs.

I've built on my own computer, not on the work side, but I've been building out, I like gems for more technical stuff and GPTs for the more fluffy stuff, if you excuse the phrase, but maybe less heavy.
Yeah.
And I've just built one in Gems, which creates full funnel client workflows and budgets for paid, and it'll do it all in one thing.
And it's pretty accurate.
Nice.
I'm quite, I was quite proud of it.
Yeah, I mean, that sounds, that sounds great.
Yeah, so, yeah, that would be amazing.
Yeah, we definitely love experimentation.
I if share, I think I've only got a pro, but I can maybe see if I can share the link to you if you wanted to play around.
Bye.
Bye.
Yeah, that'd be cool.
send you the text and then you can just paste it into your gem or whatever.
Yeah, perfect.
Awesome.
Those are the kind of things that I like playing with.
As someone will say, hey, I've got this problem.
And I'm like, hmm, maybe I can make a thing and fix the thing.
Yeah, for sure.
And honestly, we are missing some of that.
So right now we are kind of trying to optimize for the people who are able to recognize something's not working.
But like, oh, but like, maybe I can figure it out.
Like, that is, yeah, that is exactly what we're looking for.
Okay.
I like that.
I like being able to, I mean, obviously within parameters, right?
But I like to be able to play around with different ideas and see what works.
And then, you know, throw it out to other people in the company and be like, I've tried it and I think it's great.
Can you try it and tell me what's wrong with it?
Because, you know, it's mine.
So obviously I think it's perfect.
For sure.
For sure.
Awesome.
Well, yeah, I mean, so, yeah, I do, I do have to jump to another call pretty shortly.
But yeah, was there just, yeah, any other just last questions or?
I don't think so.
I think that's pretty much it.
I was just wondering what next steps would be.
Yeah, yeah, for sure.
Yeah, so I need to sync with the rest of the team, but because you've already spoken with our recruiter and with Matthew, is that correct?
Yeah.
Okay, perfect.
Yeah, so then after this, we'll, it's, it'll be a take home exercise.
And then we'll also just sort of like, meet as a team to like review that.
And then, so yeah, we're definitely toward, toward the end of the process.
And yeah, but yeah.
And then I think, yeah, Marilia will be the one to follow up with you afterward.
Okay, perfect.
Thank you very much.
Yeah, thank you so much, Georgina.
was, yeah.
Really great chatting.
It's good to meet you.
Have a good day.
Thank you.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing Georgina Ford for a director role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=304.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Proactive Problem-Solver:** Georgina Ford has a 20-year record of never missing a deadline, personally completing work to ensure delivery. She previously built custom content workflows in Asana and Ravetree to fix chaotic project management.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=619.0)
  - [**AI-First Strategy:** GrowthX is an "AI workflow company" pivoting from services to a product-first model. Its core offering is the Atlas AI platform, with human strategy as an add-on.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1373.0)
  - [**Growth Barriers:** GrowthX's main challenges are perfecting the Atlas product (which currently requires significant manual editing) and optimizing the Delivery team's structure to reduce workflow overlap and free up time.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1865.0)
  - [**Culture Fit:** Ford's self-directed experimentation (e.g., building a GPT to vet leads) directly aligns with GrowthX's need for team members who can identify and solve problems independently.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=2237.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Strategy & Challenges

  - [**Core Identity:** An "AI workflow company" pivoting from a services agency to a product-first model.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1373.0)
  - [**Product Offering:** The Atlas AI platform is the core product, with human strategy and account management as an add-on.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1373.0)
  - [**Growth Barriers:**](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1865.0)
      - [**Product Maturity:** Atlas requires significant manual editing and troubleshooting, limiting team efficiency.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1865.0)
      - [**Team Structure:** The Delivery team's structure has workflow overlap, consuming time that could be used for higher-level strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1969.0)
  - [**Performance Measurement:**](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1239.0)
      - [**Client Health:** Tracked via an "account health matrix" evaluating relationship, quality, and performance.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1239.0)
      - [**AI Performance:** Measured using Scrunch (a competitor to Profound) to track prompts and LLM referrals via GA4/GSC.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1482.0)
      - [**Reporting:** Currently uses Looker; an in-house performance dashboard is in development to centralize reporting.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1482.0)

### Candidate Experience & Problem-Solving

  - [**Current Role:** Senior Account Director at Unreal Digital, managing \~20 clients (e.g., Google Cloud, AMD).](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=341.0)
  - [**Project Management:**](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=390.0)
      - [**Prioritization:** Focuses on high-value clients.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=425.0)
      - [**Tools:** Built custom content workflows in Asana and Ravetree to fix chaotic project management.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=492.0)
      - [**Process:** A 5-day client feedback window and a 5-day creative turnaround.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=532.0)
      - [**Accountability:** Uses shareable Asana timelines to show clients how their delays impact the schedule.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=573.0)
  - [**Quality Control:**](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=692.0)
      - [**Burj Khalifa Launch:** Led a 15-person creative team in an overnight effort to rebrand all collateral after the building's name changed.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=713.0)
      - [**Client Dissatisfaction:** Resolves issues with live calls to co-write content, ensuring the client's vision is met.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=840.0)
  - [**Experimentation:**](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=2096.0)
      - [**Lead Vetting:** Built a GPT to vet leads from a syndication partner against an ICP, an experiment that is still a work in progress.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=1124.0)
      - [**Workflow Automation:** Built a personal GPT to create full-funnel client workflows and budgets.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=2171.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Megan Dickey:** Sync with the team to review the interview.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=2296.0)
  - [**Marilia:** Follow up with Georgina Ford to provide a take-home exercise.](https://fathom.video/share/Z5666extM4ZNrWCyF3mpavnnCrsjjzAu?tab=summary&timestamp=2296.0)

action_items.description: Email Megan GPT/Gem prompt text (full-funnel workflows + budgets)
Sync w/ team re: Georgina; then send take-home exercise
